questions.question: Explain the difference between exit() and _exit() system calls in process termination.
keyPoints: exit() calls exit handlers registered by atexit(), flushes stdio buffers before termination
_exit() terminates immediately without any cleanup, calling the kernel directly
_exit() is the underlying system call used by exit() after cleanup

questions.question: What is a zombie process? How can it be prevented?
keyPoints: Zombie is a terminated process whose exit status hasn't been read by parent
It remains in process table until parent calls wait()
Prevention: parent must call wait()/waitpid() or handle SIGCHLD signal
Or use non-blocking waitpid with WNOHANG option

questions.question: Describe how orphan processes are handled in Unix/Linux systems.
keyPoints: Orphan processes have terminated parents
They are adopted by init process (PID 1)
Init periodically calls wait() to reap them
This prevents orphans from becoming zombies

questions.question: Explain the role of wait() system call in process management.
keyPoints: Suspends parent until a child terminates
Retrieves child's exit status and PID
Allows parent to collect child's termination information
Enables kernel to free the child's PCB after wait()

questions.question: What are exit handlers? How are they registered?
keyPoints: Functions executed automatically when process calls exit()
Registered using atexit() function
Multiple handlers can be registered, called in reverse order
Useful for cleanup tasks like flushing logs, closing files

questions.question: Differentiate between SIGTERM and SIGKILL signals.
keyPoints: SIGTERM (15) is a polite termination request - can be caught/handled
SIGKILL (9) forces immediate termination - cannot be caught/ignored
SIGTERM allows graceful cleanup, SIGKILL may leave resources
Use SIGTERM first, SIGKILL as last resort

questions.question: Explain the process termination sequence in an operating system.
keyPoints: Process completes execution or receives termination signal
Kernel changes process state to TERMINATED
Exit status is saved in PCB
Resources (memory, files) are deallocated
Process remains in zombie state until parent calls wait()
Parent reads exit status, then PCB is removed

questions.question: Discuss in detail the various process termination causes and the kernel actions during process termination.
keyPoints: Normal termination: process calls exit() or returns from main()
Abnormal termination: receiving fatal signals (SIGSEGV, SIGFPE)
Parent termination: process becomes orphan or is killed
Kernel actions: state change to TERMINATED, save exit status, close file descriptors, deallocate memory, release CPU, remove from scheduler, retain PCB for parent wait()
After parent wait(): remove PCB from system

questions.question: Explain the parent-child relationship in process termination with special reference to zombie and orphan processes. Also explain how init process helps in handling orphans.
keyPoints: Parent must collect child's exit status using wait()
If parent doesn't wait: child becomes zombie - PCB remains in kernel
If parent dies before child: child becomes orphan - adopted by init
Init process (PID 1) periodically calls wait() to reap orphans
This prevents zombie accumulation in the system
Proper programming requires parents to handle child termination

questions.question: Write and explain a C program demonstrating process creation, termination, and zombie process handling using fork(), wait(), and exit() system calls.
keyPoints: Use fork() to create child process
Child should do some work and call exit() with status
Parent should call wait() to collect status
Demonstrate proper termination handling
Explain each system call's role
Show how zombie is prevented by wait()

questions.question: Describe the different states a process goes through during its lifecycle, with emphasis on the terminated state. Also explain when the Process Control Block (PCB) is deallocated.
keyPoints: Process states: NEW, READY, RUNNING, BLOCKED, TERMINATED
In TERMINATED state: process completed but exit status not collected
PCB contains: process ID, state, registers, memory info, file descriptors, exit status
PCB remains until parent calls wait()
After wait(): kernel removes PCB and frees PID
If parent doesn't wait: zombie process - PCB stays indefinitely
